data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0902 ET - Oil futures are giving back some of yesterday's gains but remain supported by risk premium centered around Iran and fading expectations for a nuclear deal with the U.S. Traders are taking near-term profits despite escalating tensions in the Middle East, Spartan Capital's Peter Cardillo says in a note. "However, oil prices have recently turned a corner due to geopolitical concerns, leading to a technical rebound." WTI is off 1.5% at $67.11 a barrel and Brent is down 1.4% at $68.77. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0438 ET - BP has scope to lower capital expenditure in a down-cycle, RBC Capital Markets analysts Biraj Borkhataria and Adnan Dhanani write. They write this after hosting a virtual call with BP's Chief Executive Murray Auchincloss. BP is currently guiding for capex of $14.5 billion in 2025, of which $13.8 billion is organic spend--which includes spending in existing operations--, they add. BP could remove around $500 million from its exploration budget, around $1 billion from major projects that have not yet been sanctioned, and has flexibility in its U.S. onshore business where annual capex is around $2.4 billion, they write. Shares trade up 1.6% at 379.75 pence.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0404 ET - Oil prices are likely to stay volatile, according to Ahmad Assiri, research strategist at Pepperstone in a commentary. Markets are focused on geopolitical tensions and the global economic backdrop, the strategist says. "The interplay between geopolitical manoeuvring and underlying demand strength suggests that oil market dynamics will remain prone to swings," he says. Traders should factor in the likelihood of volatility tied to shifts in regional risk, he adds. Front-month WTI crude oil futures are 1.1% lower at $67.40/bbl; front-month Brent crude futures are 1.1% lower at $68.98/bbl. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

0400 ET - BP could announce a flurry of disposals in the second half of the year, RBC Capital Markets analysts Biraj Borkhataria and Adnan Dhanani write. They write this after hosting a virtual call with BP's Chief Executive Murray Auchincloss. In the near term, the focus is on disposing infrastructure assets and some deals could be announced relatively soon, they say. Work on the Gelsenkirken refinery disposal continues while the Lightsource process is due to launch imminently, they add. Overall, BP is confident it can reach its $20 billion disposal target, the analysts write. Shares trade up 1.7% at 380 pence. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0330 ET - European oil stocks led the gains in initial trade in Europe. The stocks were boosted by Wednesday's jump in oil prices, which left Brent crude trading around $69.24 a barrel Thursday morning. Geopolitical fears in the Middle East propelled Wednesday's rise. In London, BP trades up 1.7% while Shell is up 1.26%. France's TotalEnergies rises 1.9%, Italy's Eni 1.6% and Spain's Repsol 1.35%. Portugal's Galp is up 1.46% and Norway's Equinor climbs 1.7%.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0238 ET - Medco Energi Internasional may benefit from new production by two oil-and-gas fields in the South Natuna Sea Block B at Indonesia's Riau Islands starting in 2Q, Nomura's Ahmad Maghfur Usman says in a note. Another tailwind is the newly signed multiparty gas swap agreement, which is expected to improve its overall sales by optimizing asset allocation, the analyst says. An earnings improvement at associate Amman Mineral Internasional following a smelter's ramp-up is a third tailwind. However, the Indonesian oil-and-gas company's 1Q earnings missed Nomura's expectations. The brokerage lowers the stock's target price to IDR1,700.00 from IDR2,000.00, with an unchanged buy rating. Verdhana Sekuritas analysts contributed to Nomura's report. Shares are 1.2% higher at IDR1,295.00. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0220 ET - A geopolitical risk premium hasn't yet been priced into oil markets, says the ANZ Research team in a note. Markets have largely brushed off such risks, such as tensions rising in the Middle East between Iran and Israel, and the U.S.'s partial evacuation of its embassy in Iraq, they say. These events, however, significantly raise the risks of supply disruptions. ANZ is also watching the critical minerals industry, which is central to energy systems. The U.S.-China trade war continues to pose challenges to the U.S. energy sector, they add. Front-month WTI crude oil futures are down 0.6% at $67.76/bbl; Brent crude oil futures are 0.6% lower at $69.32/bbl. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

0143 ET - The crude oil market is set to be in surplus by 4Q, which may extend into 2026, Morgan Stanley analysts say in a research note. Brent may fall to mid-$50s per barrel by 1H 2026. Elevated OPEC spare capacity after production cuts suggests that it retains ample ability to quickly ramp up production, which has acted as a drag on prices, they say. This recent buildup in spare capacity has dampened crude oil's traditional response as a geopolitical hedge. "Amid greater-than-ample supply, the market has focused on signals tracking real-time growth expectations for the U.S., the world's largest consumer of oil," they say. Front-month WTI crude oil futures are down 0.5% at $67.82/bbl; Brent crude oil futures are 0.6% lower at $69.37/bbl.(sherry.qin@wsj.com)

1500 ET - Oil futures extend gains to recover "Liberation Day" levels as the U.S. and China reach a tentative trade agreement. Other bullish news included doubts about the U.S. and Iran reaching a nuclear deal, a lower than expected U.S. inflation reading, and the EIA's report of a bigger-than-expected 3.6 million barrel draw in U.S. crude stocks as refineries processed more oil, notes Mizuho's Robert Yawger. The 621,000 barrel-a-day drop in U.S. crude exports wasn't surprising considering the narrow discount to Brent, he says. "A refiner in Rotterdam is not going to sail a boat all the way to the U.S. and get beat on cost of carry ... when they could buy barrels closer to home," Yawger adds. WTI settles up 4.9% at $68.15 a barrel, and Brent rises 4.3% to $69.77. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1248 ET - Fitch lowers its 2025 outlook for the global oil and gas sector to deteriorating from neutral, given reduced global oil demand growth on weaker economic prospects in the wake of U.S. tariff announcements. Fitch cited "quicker-than-expected unwinding of OPEC+ voluntary production cuts, and increasing non-OPEC+ output" as reasons for the move. "We now assume global oil demand will grow by about 800,000 barrels per day this year, compared with our previous expectations of slightly over 1 million bpd. The market will remain oversupplied in 2025 due to faster supply growth," Fitch says.Natural gas prices are expected to support producers' cash flows, which could somewhatoffset the impact of declining oil prices, Fitch says. (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0635 ET - Palm oil edged higher amid soybean oil's gains overnight and potential bargain hunting. The tropical oil's prices were supported by potential bargain hunting following the recent decline as well as optimism over demand recovery from India, Kenanga Futures says in a research note. However, investors remain cautious about palm oil's elevated stockpiles, which reached an eight-month high in Malaysia, they noted. Kenanga sets the support and resistance level for the August contract at 3,800 ringgit and 3,900 ringgit a ton, respectively. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for August delivery ended 1 ringgit higher at 3,840 ringgit a ton. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0415 ET - Shares in gold miners trade up as futures in the precious metal are boosted by safe-haven demand. Gold futures trade up 0.6% at $3,364.20 a troy ounce, having risen as high as $3,398.40/oz earlier in the session. Investor appetite for gold has been boosted by geopolitical uncertainty in the Middle East. In South Africa, AngloGold Ashanti rises 2.5%, Gold Fields is up 2.2% while Harmony Gold rises 1.1%. In London, Endeavour Mining rises 0.8% while gold and silver miner Fresnillo rises 0.3%.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

2132 ET - Aneka Tambang remains UOB Kay Hian's preferred stock play for gold, the brokerage's Benyamin Mikael says in a note. This view is underpinned by the Indonesian mining company's robust earnings growth, the stock's strong three-month average daily turnover of $31.8 million and foreign inflows into the stock exceeding $290 million year-to-date. The gold and nickel miner's 2025 profit could grow by 4.4% for every 5% increase in gold prices, the analyst estimates. UOB Bank maintains a positive outlook on gold, partly driven by long-term safe-haven demand. UOB Kay Hian raises the stock's target price to IDR4,600.00 from IDR3,300.00 with an unchanged buy rating. Shares last closed at IDR3,280.00. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1422 ET - Gold finishes flat as wider markets digest the implications of President Trump's announcement of a tentative trade deal with China. Beijing is putting a six-month limit on rare-earth export licenses for U.S. automakers and manufacturers, according to people familiar with the matter. Investors have been approaching today's announcement with uncertainty. Gold, which often rises when uncertainty grows, nevertheless was basically unchanged for the day. The front-month contract closed up 0.01% to $3,321.30 a troy ounce. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1445 GMT - Canadian leisure travel company Transat's Pratt & Whitney engine issues are likely going to continue for a couple of years, CFO Jean-Francois Pruneau says on an analyst call. In F2Q, the company recognized C$20 million in revenue from Pratt & Whitney for its grounded aircrafts due to ongoing engine issues. The executive says that the company will continue to receive these revenue contributions which are calculated based on the number of planes grounded multiplied by the days they are grounded. "We don't expect this situation to be settled before 2027," Pruneau says, so Transat expects additional revenue contributions over those next years. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1243 GMT - European airlines fell sharply on the news of a Boeing 787 jet operated by Air India flight had crashed in Western India shortly after takeoff. The Boeing model, known as the Dreamliner, hadn't had a fatal incident in its 14 years of operation. Deutsche Lufthansa was down 3.6%, International Consolidated Airlines lost 3.4%, Air France fell 6.3%, TUI dropped 8.9%, easyJet decreased 3.4%, Ryanair fell 2.65% and Wizz Air traded down 1.8%. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

1235 GMT - Daimler Truck's deal with Toyota to merge their Japanese truck units could unlock significant value, Bank of America Securities analysts write. The new merged company will be formed with Daimler Truck's Fuso business and Toyota's Hino unit. The bank thinks Daimler Truck's roughly 45% stake in the new combined entity could be worth around 2.7 billion euros, which equates to 10% of Daimler Truck's market capitalization. That is far above the 5% of company profits that Fuso accounts for. Daimler Truck plans to sell down to a 25% stake in Hino/Fuso once the deal closes, which could generate 1.2 billion euros in cash. "We think these proceeds could be returned to shareholders in the form of an upsized share buyback." Shares fall 1.1%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0808 GMT - DHL Group's second quarter results should show that profits have held up well despite the market's volatility, Deutsche Bank analyst Andy Chu writes. The bank is positive on DHL as it has market-leading assets, largely with high barriers to entry, and a proven ability to adapt to uncertainty. It expects free cash flow at around 3 billion euros, allowing the company to pay at least a flat dividend of 1.85 euros per share and buy back shares. "We acknowledge that DHL is a cyclical stock and has volatile earnings, but we appreciate the resilient free cash flow, which we believe supports the medium-term buy case." Deutsche Bank lifts its target price to 47 euros from 42 euros. Shares fall 0.5% to 40.78 euros. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0504 GMT - Qantas Airways' lowered capacity guidance could put pressure on the Australian carrier's annual earnings, Macquarie analysts warn. They reckon that the cut, coupled with the airline's unchanged expectations regarding revenue per available seat kilometer, implies a A$200 million reduction in revenue for the second half of the current fiscal year. There is some mitigation for earnings from lower fuel costs, they add in a note to clients. Macquarie raises its target price by 4.7% to A$10.10 and stays neutral on the stock, citing caution toward its international operations from competition. Shares are up 2.6% at A$10.775. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

0407 GMT - The overall competitive environment is unlikely to alter significantly, considering the current stance of the government and the market against excessive market competition, Nomura analysts write in a note. The analysts believe only the format of competition is likely to see some changes. While price cuts may no longer be as effective a tool as it was before, Nomura expects more new and facelift models with upgraded functions and equipment to be launched by active EV players, they say. BYD remains Nomura's sector top pick as they believe its market-leading position remains intact. The company will likely continue to maintain its edge in technology and business scale. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0102 GMT - Qantas will likely enjoy the capital-expenditure benefits of repatriating 13 Asia-based aircraft in its 2027 and 2028 fiscal years, UBS analysts reckon. They write in a note that the Australian carrier's closure of its underperforming Singapore-based JV is in keeping with its agile network management and focus on improving returns from invested capital. The closure does not materially change their view of the stock. They think that stable demand and easing fuel prices should support fiscal 2026 margin expansion. UBS raises its target price 11% to A$10.30 and keeps a neutral recommendation on the stock, which is up 4.5% at A$10.97. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

0054 GMT - Qantas's bull at Morgan Stanley is surprised that the Australian carrier hasn't flagged a boost from lower fuel costs. Analyst Andrew G. Scott tells clients in a note that he would have expected some kind of benefit from favorable moves in underlying oil prices. He thinks that Qantas's decision to close its underperforming Asia JV is prudent given structural headwinds and the ability to better use capital elsewhere. However, that is somewhat offset in his view by the scale of Jetstar Asia's losses and a cut to group-wide capacity guidance. MS raises its target price 4.3% to A$12.00 and keeps an overweight recommendation on the stock, which is up 4.6% at A$10.98. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

0011 GMT - Hanwha Ocean could get a valuation boost of up to KRW15.6 trillion, equivalent to $11.39 billion, from its entry into the U.S. shipbuilding market, say Korea Investment & Securities analysts Kang Kyung-tae and Nam Chae-min. The analysts expect the South Korean shipbuilder's value could increase by KRW12.4 trillion after raising its stake in Austal to become the largest shareholder in the Australia-based U.S. military shipbuilder. Hanwha could also get an extra KRW3.2 trillion valuation boost from its recent acquisition of a U.S. shipyard in Philadelphia, they reckon. Hanwha is valued at an estimated KRW35 trillion in total, they add. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

data.title: Britain ready to implement US tariff deal, trade minister says
data.description:
Britain is ready to implement its side of a tariff deal with the United States and is hopeful for a proclamation from U.S. President Donald Trump to put the agreement into effect in the coming days, trade minister Jonathan Reynolds said on Thursday.

British Prime Minister Keir Starmer and Trump on May 8 agreed to reduce tariffs on UK imports of cars and steel to the U.S., with Britain agreeing to lower tariffs on beef and ethanol, but implementation of the deal has been delayed.

Reynolds met U.S. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick on Tuesday and discussed the implementation of the deal. Asked on Thursday if there would be an update by the end of the week, Reynolds said he was "very hopeful".

"We're ready to go, and as soon as the president and the White House are ready to go on their side, we'll implement (our) part of the deal," Reynolds told reporters.

Reynolds said he would issue a government order known as a statutory instrument to implement the changes to reciprocal tariffs. Officials said that the update on implementation was likely to come early next week.

One of the details to be ironed out before the deal can be implemented is steel quotas. Reynolds added that he wanted to make sure the tariff reductions applied to every bit of the UK steel industry, as the U.S. finalises quotas that will place supply chain requirements on British steel exports to the United States.

The bioethanol industry has warned its future is under threat, with Associated British Foods deciding on the fate of a plant later this month. Reynolds acknowledged the deal could increase competition but said the industry was already struggling.

"We are very sensitive to the ethanol issue... (but) they're losing a lot of money already," Reynolds said, adding regulatory tweaks could help, but that for financial support: "any intervention I make has to be a clear route to profitability."

"So there are much wider issues for these partners than just the U.S. trade deal."

data.title: Norway's Central Bank May Miss Its Window to Cut Rates Next Week, Says ING
data.description:
ING said it had originally called for a June Norwegian rate cut at Norges Bank amid a worsening growth picture and signs of easing inflationary pressures.

However, inflation and growth figures have led markets and the consensus to dismiss the chance of a move next Thursday, wrote the bank in a note. The question remains open between August and September for the first rate cut.

ING thinks markets are underestimating an August move with 9bps priced in.

The bank sees dovish risks as new rate and economic projections are released at this meeting. The March update had Q3 2025 rates averaging 4.38%, suggesting one 25bps cut at the September meeting, but there might be more openness to a move already in August.

In ING's view, the conditions to cut rates in June are all there. The main reason it calls for a hold is that a central bank that has consistently surprised on the hawkish side is unlikely to deliver a surprise cut when markets and consensus have ruled it out.

However, Norges Bank may be playing a risky gamble with its excessive focus on short-term headline inflation swings, added ING. When conditions for cuts arrive, there is no guarantee the krone (NOK) will be enjoying the current stability.

NOK is the least liquid currency in G10 and the one that generally faces sharper selloffs in times of market instability, recalled the bank. So Norges Bank could face a lose-lose scenario: either cutting rates in a NOK sell-off, risking further NOK losses and higher imported inflation, or keeping rates on hold again to stabilise the currency, extending the monetary pain for the economy.

data.title: Smile Solutions of Goodlettsville Data Breach - Levi & Korsinsky, LLP Launches Investigation
data.description:
NEW YORK CITY, NY / ACCESS Newswire / June 12, 2025 / Smile Solutions of Goodlettsville recently disclosed that one of its former debt recovery vendors of 23 years, suffered a data breach that compromised the sensitive personal and protected health data of several individuals. This data breach has led to concerns over the security of sensitive personal and protected health information entrusted to Smile Solutions of Goodlettsville.

WHAT'S THIS ABOUT?

Smile Solutions of Goodlettsville was recently made aware of a data security event involving its former debt recovery vendor of 23 years, NRS. In July 2024, NRS experienced a network outage that resulted in unauthorized access to protected account information. During this breach, information provided by Smile Solutions to NRS for debt collection was accessed and is believed to have been copied.

Upon receiving notice, Smile Solutions of Goodlettsville conducted a comprehensive review of the impacted data to determine what information was compromised and identified affected individuals. The potentially impacted files contained personal information including name, address at the time of referral, date of birth, Social Security number, financial account information and relevant details of clinical services required for debt collection.

On March 28, 2025, Smile Solutions of Goodlettsville made a public disclosure and may soon begin sending out notice letters to the impacted individuals. Individuals who received notice that their personal information was compromised have rights and potential legal remedies available to them.

WHY YOU MAY BE ENTITLED TO COMPENSATION?

Data breaches are serious matters that can cause long-term damage. Hackers may use stolen information to commit identity theft, financial fraud, or other crimes. Companies that fail to secure your personal data may be held liable for the resulting harm.

HOW DO I KNOW IF I WAS AFFECTED?

If you received a data breach notification letter from Smile Solutions of Goodlettsville, you are likely affected. Follow the link below to find out if you may be eligible for compensation.

Levi Korsinsky, LLP is investigating whether affected individuals are entitled to compensation. There is no cost or obligation to participate. Follow the link below to find out:

The firm's team of over 70 extraordinary attorneys and professionals have a winning track record going against the most powerful defense attorneys in the world and know how to maximize your compensation. The firm is a 100% contingency firm - we don't get paid unless you get paid! Please visit us at www.zlk.com for more information. Attorney Advertising. Notes:

1.

Rule 8 of the Takeover Code (the "Code") sets out the circumstances in which Dealing Disclosures and/or Opening Position Disclosures are required to be made by the parties to an offer, persons acting in concert with them, persons with an interest in relevant securities of 1% or more and exempt principal traders ("EPTs") connected with the parties to an offer.

2.

Where an offeror is marked "Disclosure of dealings and positions in this offeror is not required", dealings and positions in relevant securities of that offeror are not required to be disclosed under Rule 8. This is either because it has been announced that the offer or possible offer is, or is likely to be, solely in cash or because disclosure is not required for some other reason.

3.

Dealings and positions required to be disclosed under Rule 8 of the Code include dealings and positions in derivatives referenced to, and options in respect of, relevant securities of an offeree company and, where appropriate, an offeror (see Note 2 above).

4.

The information set out in the Disclosure Table relating to the relevant securities in issue of an offeree company or of an offeror (and any amendments thereto) has been extracted from the relevant announcement made by such company under Rule 2.9 of the Code. Similarly, details of any deadline set under Rule 2.6 by which a potential offeror must announce either a firm intention to make a bid or that it does not intend to make an offer (or, in the case of Rule 2.6(a), by which the Panel must consent to an extension of the deadline) has been extracted from the relevant announcement made by the offeree company.

5.

Additions and deletions of offeree companies and offerors are reflected in the Disclosure Table throughout the day, following the relevant announcement. Other amendments may also be made throughout the day. In any event, details of the classes of relevant securities issued by an offeree or offeror company, the number of such securities in issue ("NSI") and the International Securities Identification Number ("ISIN") of such securities announced under Rule 2.9 of the Code before 5.00 pm each day will be included in the final version of the Disclosure Table by 5.30 pm that day. The final version of the Disclosure Table issued each day will be marked "Final" and persons required to make Dealing Disclosures and Opening Position Disclosures are advised to use that final version. Any changes to the Rule 2.6 deadline of a potential offeror will also be reflected in the "Final" version of the Disclosure Table, although any such changes will not be shown in the "Amendments" section at the top of the Disclosure Table.

6.

Dealing Disclosures by the parties to an offer, persons acting in concert with them and connected EPTs must be made by no later than 12 noon on the business day (as defined in the Code) following the date of the relevant dealing and Dealing Disclosures by persons with an interest in relevant securities of 1% or more must be made by 3.30 pm on the business day following the date of the relevant dealing.

7.

Opening Position Disclosures by the parties to an offer and by connected EPTs must be made by no later than 12 noon on the 10th business day after the commencement of the offer period or the announcement that first identifies an offeror (as the case may be). data.title: Levi & Korsinsky, LLP Investigates LexisNexis Risk Solutions Data Breach
data.description:
NEW YORK CITY, NY / ACCESS Newswire / June 12, 2025 / LexisNexis Risk Solutions recently disclosed that it suffered a data breach through its third-party software development platform that compromised the sensitive personal data of individuals. This data breach has led to concerns over the security of sensitive personal and protected health information entrusted to LexisNexis Risk Solutions.

WHAT'S THIS ABOUT?

On December 25, 2024, LexisNexis Risk Solutions became aware of a security incident on its third-party software development platform. The issue did not affect LNRS's own networks or systems. Upon detection, LNRS launched an investigation with the assistance of third-party cyber security experts to determine the nature and scope of the incident.

The investigation determined that an unauthorized third party gained access to certain LNRS data from the third-party platform. LNRS conducted a comprehensive review of the impacted data to determine what information was compromised and identified affected individuals. The potentially impacted files contained personal information including name, contact information (such as phone number, postal or email address), Social Security number, driver's license number, or date of birth. No financial or credit card information was affected.

On May 24, 2025, LNRS began sending notice letters to impacted individuals and on May 27, 2024filed a notice with the Maine Attorney General's Office. Compensation may be available for those individuals who received notice that their personal information was compromised.

WHY YOU MAY BE ENTITLED TO COMPENSATION?

Data breaches are serious matters that can cause long-term damage. Hackers may use stolen information to commit identity theft, financial fraud, or other crimes. Companies that fail to secure your personal data may be held liable for the resulting harm.

data.title: Global Commodities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Commodities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1209 ET - Base metal prices rise, with LME three-month copper up 0.6% at $9,702.0 a metric ton and LME three-month aluminum up 0.2% at $2,520.50 a ton. Metals have seen buying interest on the back of U.S. dollar weakness, which makes it cheaper for international purchasers to buy dollar-denominated commodities. That said, strong growth in Chinese refined production and the hit to demand from U.S. tariffs have damped the outlook for copper prices in the second half of the year, BNP Paribas Asset Management analysts say in a note. On the other hand, limited Chinese aluminum domestic supply growth will tighten the Chinese market from the second half, boosting Chinese primary aluminum imports, analysts say. This will tighten the market outside China and in turn provide some price support, though U.S. tariffs are a key risk, analysts say. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

1203 ET - Gold futures rise on tariff tensions and geopolitical uncertainty. Futures are up 2% at $3,409.90 a troy ounce. The precious metal has jumped after President Trump threatened to impose unilateral tariffs in the coming weeks, keeping trade anxiety and safe-haven demand elevated, Tradu.com's Nikos Tzabouras says in a note. At the same time, Trump has signaled low confidence in nuclear negotiations with Iran, adding to geopolitical fears, Tzabouras says. Weakness in the U.S. dollar and persistent central bank purchases of bullion have further amplified gold's strength. Still, Iran and the U.S. are set to resume talks over the weekend and U.S. officials have hinted at delays in tariffs on key negotiating partners, Tzabouras writes. These developments could temper gold's safe-haven appeal, but broader market sentiment favors gold's upside, he adds. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

1115 ET - Lean hog futures on the CME are up again in early trading. Hogs have rise in the last four sessions. That's despite a steep drop-off in pork export sales reported by the USDA in this week's export sales report, with only 9,700 metric tons reported sold for the week ended June 5. That's off 70% from the prior 4-week average, according to the USDA. But exports shipped for the week totaled 33,800 tons, which is up 25% from the previous average, also an important fact in the report according to Ben Johnson of New Frontier Capital Markets in a note. CME hog futures are up 0.4%, and live cattle is down 0.4%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1056 ET - U.S. natural gas inventories increased by 109 billion cubic feet last week to 2,707 Bcf, and were 139 Bcf or 5.4% above the five-year average for the time of year, the EIA reports. The storage injection was in line with the estimate in a Wall Street Journal survey of analysts, and above the average build for the week of 87 Bcf. The deficit against the year-ago storage level narrowed to 256 Bcf from 288 Bcf the week before. Nymex natural gas futures are up 2.1% at $3.582/mmBtu after three straight sessions of losses. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1050 ET - Data updated by the U.S. Drought Monitor today shows that soil in Nebraska is receiving much-needed rainfall--with areas of extreme or exceptional drought disappearing over the past week. Most of the state is now experiencing abnormal dryness or moderate drought, which is less of a factor in Nebraska than in other states as many Nebraskan farmers use irrigation systems to water crops--more so than in other states. CBOT grains are tentative in early trading, with corn up 0.5%, soybeans flat, and wheat flat. The WASDE report will be published at noon eastern time.(kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1021 ET - The NOAA's Climate Prediction Center says that there's an 82% chance of an ENSO-neutral climate condition from June to August. But the odds of it extending into the winter are less, with a 48% chance of a neutral climate in November through January, and a 41% chance of a La Niña climate at that time. A neutral climate over the summer may be supportive for grains growing in the Corn Belt, with rain versus heat being potentially more balanced. Earlier this week, the USDA reported that U.S. crop conditions were steadily improving. 71% of the corn crop was in good or excellent condition, up two points from the previous week. Good and excellent soybeans rose one point, to 68%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1013 ET - An interest rate cut by the Fed looks increasingly likely, helping keep the dollar in check. The currency has already been under pressure as Trump realigns the U.S. geopolitical leadership and pushes his aggressive tariffs agenda. On top of that, data pointing to slowing inflation and an easing labor market increase the odds that Fed cuts could come by early fall or even sooner. Lower interest rates would be one less reason for foreign investors to buy U.S. assets. The WSJ Dollar Index falls 0.6%, after hitting a new 52-week intraday low earlier today. The greenback weakens 0.8% against the yen and the euro and nearly 1% versus the Swiss franc. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1003 ET - Grain futures on the CBOT are mixed, with traders seen as unwilling to take a strong position in row crop futures ahead of today's WASDE. For corn, the uncertainty comes after record ethanol production was reported by the EIA yesterday. "There was good news with a near record week of ethanol production last week, but that good news had little effect on prices," says Tomm Pfitzenmaier of Summit Commodity Brokerage in a note. "When a market doesn't/can't react positively to good news, that is not a good sign for prices." Soybeans and wheat are also tentative in early trading, with soybeans getting only slight support from strong soybean oil futures. Soybeans are up 0.2%, and wheat is flat. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0952 ET - The risk of a full-scale conflict between Israel and Iran continues to hang over the oil market even if it isn't an immediate base-case scenario, says Saxo's Ole Hansen. "Tensions have steadily escalated over Iran's nuclear ambitions, and Israel along with the U.S. have long maintained that they will not allow Iran to develop a nuclear weapon--a red line that may drive Israel toward unilateral military action if diplomacy fails," the head of commodity strategy says. Iran said Thursday that it would soon open a third uranium enrichment site--a major escalation of its nuclear program that comes as President Trump seeks to halt Tehran's production of fissile material as part of a nuclear deal. Brent crude is down 1.2% to $68.96 a barrel, while WTI falls 1.1% to $67.40 a barrel. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0936 ET - U.S. natural gas futures are higher after three days of losses, helped by warmer temperature forecasts and a pickup in LNG demand. Maintenance at numerous LNG terminals has contributed to recent price weakness and large storage builds. Warmer conditions developing across the western U.S. will likely drive up demand for power generation. "This could coincide with returning liquefaction availability, providing momentum for a Henry Hub price rebound toward the $4 mark," Christoph Halser at Rystad Energy says in a note. Nymex natural gas is up 2.5% at $3.594/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0935 ET - Base metal prices are mixed, with LME three-month copper up 0.3% at $9,676.50 a metric ton and LME three-month aluminum down 0.4% at $2,507.50 a ton. The surprise doubling of U.S. aluminum tariffs to 50% last week has led to a huge amount of uncertainty in the industry, JPMorgan analysts say in a note. The 50% tariff doesn't appear to be fully factored into the U.S. Midwest premium over LME prices, they write. While existing inventory in the U.S. can form a near-term buffer, it will quickly run down. The aluminum premium appears to be in a state of paralysis, given lingering uncertainty around whether the 50% tariff will eventually be walked back or if key exemptions reduce pressure, JPM says. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0925 ET - Brent crude is unlikely to hold near $70 a barrel beyond the peak summer demand season, Saxo's Ole Hansen says. "Crude oil markets remain caught in a tug-of-war between bullish near-term demand and rising geopolitical risks on one hand, and macroeconomic headwinds and rising OPEC+ production on the other," says the head of commodity strategy. Summer consumption is tightening supplies, with refiners racing to meet surging gasoline and diesel demand. However, the short-term squeeze is expected to be offset by rising OPEC+ output. Meanwhile, traders face a complex backdrop of geopolitical risks and economic uncertainty driven by global trade tensions, Hansen says. Brent currently trades 1.5% lower at $68.76 a barrel. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

data.title: Update: Market Chatter: Boeing Nearing Deal for Royal Air Maroc Fleet Renewal
data.description:
(Updates with response from an Airbus spokesperson in the fourth paragraph.)

Boeing is close to entering a deal with Royal Air Maroc to supply the airline with dozens of its jets as Morocco's flagship carrier seeks to renew its fleet, Reuters reported Wednesday, citing industry sources.

The airline is expected to stick with Boeing for most of its needs, including the 737 MAX and 787 Dreamliner, the sources reportedly said.

Royal Air Maroc is also ordering 20 A220 jets from Airbus, opening its door to the French planemaker for the first time in almost 25 years, the report said, citing the sources.

"We don't comment on conversations we may or may not be having with our customers," an Airbus spokesperson said in a statement emailed to MT Newswires.

Boeing didn't immediately respond to a request for comment from MT Newswires, while Royal Air Maroc couldn't be reached for comment.

data.title: Kennedy's new vaccine adviser was expert witness against Merck vaccine
data.description:
Key points:
  • Kulldorff served as expert witness against Merck's Gardasil
  • Kennedy fired previous vaccine advisers citing drug industry conflicts
  • CDC rules say advisers can't be expert witness in vaccine cases

One of the new vaccine advisers picked by U.S. Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. has earned thousands of dollars as an expert witness in litigation against Merck’s Gardasil vaccine, court records show.

Martin Kulldorff, a biostatistician and epidemiologist who publicly criticized COVID-era lockdowns, is one of eight new members named by Kennedy on Wednesday to the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices, a highly influential panel that recommends which shots should be administered to the American public.

Kennedy fired the entire previous 17-member committee of expert vaccine advisers this week, claiming they were "plagued with persistent conflicts of interest" from financial ties to drugmakers. The departing experts say their work was subjected to rigorous vetting and rules for recusal for any conflict.

Kulldorff recently served as an expert witness for plaintiffs who accused Merck of concealing the risks of Gardasil, a vaccine for the prevention of cancers caused by human papillomavirus (HPV). In March, a federal judge in North Carolina ruled in favor of Merck in one of those cases that included about 200 lawsuits.

At a deposition in October, Kulldorff testified that the plaintiffs paid him $400 an hour and he had already billed for about $33,000 in legal work on the case through late September. He said he also received a $4,000 retainer in the North Carolina case, according to court documents.

Kulldorff is also listed as an expert witness in a similar case pending against Merck in Los Angeles state court, records show.

Under ACIP’s rules, committee members cannot serve as a "paid litigation consultant or expert witness in litigation involving a vaccine manufacturer" during their tenure on the panel.

Prior work as an expert witness against drugmakers may require a waiver from the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention and recusal from votes involving Merck and HPV vaccines. The agency has said in its rules it "will generally consider issuance of waivers in specific situations."

Kulldorff and plaintiffs lawyers in the Merck case did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Merck declined to comment.

A spokesman for Kennedy's Health and Human Services Department said on Wednesday that "all newly appointed ACIP members were thoroughly vetted." HHS did not immediately respond to a request for comment on Kulldorff.

KENNEDY AND GARDASIL

Before joining the Trump administration, Kennedy was a longtime plaintiffs’ lawyer and played an instrumental role in organizing mass litigation against Merck over its Gardasil vaccine, which brought in sales of $8.58 billion in 2024.

The shot is recommended as a routine immunization for 11 and 12-year-olds by the CDC to prevent cervical and certain head and neck cancers caused by the virus.

Kennedy’s work on the Gardasil case drew attention from Congress during the confirmation process for health secretary. Kennedy said he would divest his financial interest in that litigation to his non-dependent, adult son.

In a post on X, Kennedy praised Kulldorff as a "leading expert in vaccine safety and infectious disease surveillance." He also cited his previous service as a government adviser, including on a vaccine safety subgroup assisting the full ACIP board.

Kulldorff gained prominence during the COVID-19 pandemic as a co-author of the Great Barrington Declaration in October 2020, which called on public health officials to roll back lockdowns, arguing they were causing irreparable harm. One of his co-authors was Dr. Jay Bhattacharya, who now serves under Kennedy as National Institutes of Health director.

Kulldorff wrote a 29-page report on Gardasil that was filed on January 6 in the North Carolina case. His conclusion was that Merck had "not done an appropriate job evaluating potential safety problems" related to the shot. He also highlighted his prior service on ACIP’s vaccine safety working groups.

Gardasil was approved in the U.S. in 2006 after going through clinical trials to prove its safety and efficacy.

"I have served on two CDC ACIP vaccine safety working groups, concerning the measles-mumps-rubella-varicella (MMRV) and COVID vaccines," Kulldorff wrote.

He also wrote in his litigation report that he developed some of the methods that are used "by FDA and CDC in their routine post-market vaccine safety surveillance work."

data.title: Omega Flex Executive Chairman Kevin R Hoben Sells Shares
data.description:
Reporter NameHoben Kevin R
RelationshipExecutive Chairman
TypeSell
Amount$30,666
SEC FilingForm 4

Omega Flex's Executive Chairman, Kevin R Hoben, sold 972 shares of Common Stock on June 12, 2025, at a price of $31.55 per share, totaling $30,666. Following the transaction, Hoben directly owns 898,533 shares and indirectly owns 340 shares through a 401(k) plan.

SEC Filing: Omega Flex, Inc. [ OFLX ] - Form 4 - Jun. 12, 2025

data.title: Conagra Brands Stock Falls After Downgrade. Rising Meat Prices Are to Blame. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Nate Wolf

Conagra Brands stock was down Thursday after analysts at BofA Securities reduced their outlook for the packaged food maker, citing the rising cost of meat.

Peter T. Galbo and Yasmine Deswandhy at BofA downgraded Conagra stock to Underperform from Neutral and lowered their price target to $20 from $27 in a research note Thursday. Conagra, which owns brands like Slim Jim, Chef Boyardee, and Hebrew National, could see its product expenses climb considerably in fiscal 2026, the analysts said.

Shares were down 1.5% to $22.03, putting the stock on pace for a 52-week closing low, according to Dow Jones Market Data.

Meat prices were the main culprit for the downgrade. Galbo and Deswandhy now estimate a 6.7% weighted growth in overall costs-of-goods-sold over the next year, headlined by 25% price growth in animal proteins, namely chicken, beef, and pork.

"Protein commodity costs have re-accelerated," the pair wrote. "And we find it difficult for CAG to fully offset these pressures through either pricing, other revenue growth management initiatives, or productivity."

Conagra can pass through some additional costs to consumers, Galbo and Deswandhy noted, but shoppers are unlikely to accept the kind of price hikes Conagra will need to protect its profits. And the company's single-serve frozen meals business — its largest category — is most vulnerable to consumers balking at price increases, they found.

"Consumers more than before are seeking value both for at-home and away-from-home consumption," the BofA analysts note. The pair lowered their 2026 fiscal-year earnings forecast for Conagra to $2 a share, down from $2.37 a share.

Conagra, which has seen its shares slump 21% so far in 2025, reports earnings for its fiscal fourth quarter on July 10.

Write to Nate Wolf at nate.wolf@barrons.com

data.title: Base Metal Prices Rise on Weaker U.S. Dollar — Market Talk
data.description: Base metal prices rise, with LME three-month copper up 0.6% at $9,702.0 a metric ton and LME three-month aluminum up 0.2% at $2,520.50 a ton. Metals have seen buying interest on the back of U.S. dollar weakness, which makes it cheaper for international purchasers to buy dollar-denominated commodities. That said, strong growth in Chinese refined production and the hit to demand from U.S. tariffs have damped the outlook for copper prices in the second half of the year, BNP Paribas Asset Management analysts say in a note. On the other hand, limited Chinese aluminum domestic supply growth will tighten the Chinese market from the second half, boosting Chinese primary aluminum imports, analysts say. This will tighten the market outside China and in turn provide some price support, though U.S. tariffs are a key risk, analysts say. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)
